prompt: Our open-source project gets plenty of stars but very few contributors. We want to add a nudge in the README and the issue templates that encourages first-time contributors without sounding desperate. The project is a CLI tool for database migrations.

Our enterprise sales team needs a follow-up email template for prospects who attended a demo but went silent for 2 weeks. It should re-engage without being pushy. The product is an observability platform.

We have an internal engineering blog that nobody reads even though leadership asks people to post. Write a Slack bot message that nudges engineers to write a post after they ship a major feature, framing it as a natural extension of the work they already did.

Our CI pipeline takes 45 minutes but there's a fast-path flag that most developers don't know about. Design a nudge system that suggests the fast path when it detects the change is small and low-risk, without being wrong so often that people ignore it.

We need an in-app message for users who have been on the free plan for 90+ days and use >80% of the free tier limits. The upgrade nudge should feel like a value proposition, not a paywall threat. Include a manipulation audit for this one.

A developer tool we maintain has excellent keyboard shortcuts that power users love but new users never discover. Design a progressive disclosure system that introduces shortcuts at moments when the user would benefit, without creating tutorial fatigue.

Our HR team wants to nudge managers to complete quarterly reviews on time. Last quarter 40% were late. Design the notification sequence — timing, channel, and copy — that gets compliance up without making it feel like nagging from HR.

We're sunsetting a popular feature (custom themes) because maintenance cost is too high. We need to communicate this to users who actively use themes in a way that validates their preference while redirecting them to the new design system. This is sensitive — some users are very attached.
